learning_outcomes.description: Understand the development and relationship of distinct disciplinary approaches to studying motion pictures as art.
Use the terms, concepts, and arguments of a particular scholarly approach to develop original claims and analyses.
Critically evaluate pieces of scholarly writing to demonstrate understanding of different perspectives and approaches and their potential merits, constraints, and flaws.
Demonstrate understanding of the fundamental attributes of and issues related to aesthetic criticism and evaluation and apply such understanding to set films.
Demonstrate capacity to produce original criticism and develop one’s own critical voice.

assessments.task_description: Your task in this assessment is to evaluate a short sequence of film from one of the movies shown in the first 5 weeks of the course by writing a piece of criticism about it. This sequence must be no more that 1 minute long (it can be shorter). Your evaluation must be informed by your studies during the course, including course readings and any wider reading that you have done. We will issue detailed guidelines in the early weeks of the course.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: You are required to write a piece of criticism that demonstrates your strengths in the evaluation of film. Some essay suggestions will be released mid-way through the semester, and you may wish to consult with the course coordinator about designing your own essay topic.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description: The aim of the in-class writing task is to give you the opportunity to practice the evaluation of film by writing criticism about a short sequence that will be shown in the tutorial.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submitted in tutorials. Handwritten on A4 sheet of paper. No electronic aid can be used.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK ONE: CRITERIA AND CRITICISM

This week introduces you to the course, and will consider one of the ways in which the evaluation of film (and many other arts) takes place, that is, through the insistence on medium specific criteria. We will explore what that means with reference to the film Don't Look Now.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK ONE: CRITERIA AND CRITICISM

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK TWO: V F PERKINS AND THE LIMITS OF CRITICISM

V. F. Perkins is a major figure in the development of film theory and criticism and this week will explore the ways his work offers an alternative to the criteria-based evaluation we looked at in the first week. Perkins's writing on Max Ophuls's Letter from an Unknown Woman remains some of the finest criticism (in a close reading mode) ever written and we will look at both carefully.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK TWO: V F PERKINS AND THE LIMITS OF CRITICISM

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK THREE: AESTHETIC EXPERIENCE AS THEME AND SPECTACLE - FIRST EXAMPLE

We might argue that criticism is a kind of report of aesthetic experience and in the next two weeks we will look at movies which attempt in various ways to depict aesthetic experience as well as other kinds of artistic activity and consumption. Tom Ford was a notable fashion designer before directing movies and Nocturnal Animals is his adaptation of a novel by Austin Wright called Tony and Susan and it is the example we will look at closely in order to think about how showing something that at first sight seems emphatically 'inner' like aesthetic experience, has to conjure a range of 'outer' forms in order to be legible.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK THREE: AESTHETIC EXPERIENCE AS THEME AND SPECTACLE - FIRST EXAMPLE

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK FOUR: AESTHETIC EXPERIENCE AS THEME AND SPECTACLE - SECOND EXAMPLE

Last week we saw how aesthetic experience can be recruited as an aspect of a film's plot and drama as well as its own aesthetic shape. Celine Sciamma's 2019 film Portrait of a Lady on Fire seems to go a step further by directly depicting the artist at work and entangling this with a romance plot. The result is a strange and compelling film that mixes together flows of feeling - for art, community, music, and love - in a way that insists on the primacy of beauty amidst discouraging social and historical conditions. In other words it is a film of significant ambition when it comes to the spectacle of art and beauty and aesthetics. To what extent is it successful?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK FOUR: AESTHETIC EXPERIENCE AS THEME AND SPECTACLE - SECOND EXAMPLE

Celine Sciamma's 2019 film Portrait of a Lady on Fire seems to go a step further by directly depicting the artist at work and entangling this with a romance plot. The result is a strange and compelling film that mixes together flows of feeling -  for art, community, music, and love - in a way that insists on the primacy of beauty amidst discouraging social and historical conditions. In other words it is a film of significant ambition when it comes to the spectacle of art and beauty and aesthetics. To what extent is it successful?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK FIVE: THE FIGURE OF THE ARTIST - FIRST EXAMPLE

The history of art teaches us that one of the major topics that emerges is the role and nature of the artist - we might call it a modernist (although it predates modernism by centuries) gesture, that is to recruit the making and the medium of making as a theme in the artwork itself. Put more simply: what do we get and how do we evaluate those works which make artistic consciousness central? Paul Thomas Anderson's Phantom Thread is about a couturier named Reynolds Woodcock who seems at the height of success and is shown as a very controlling and exacting character. The plot of the film turns on the extent to which that control is, in many ways, out of his hands. As with Portrait of a Lady on Fire we will practice evaluating both this theme and the extent to which it is realised with wit, intelligence and dramatic force.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK FIVE: THE FIGURE OF THE ARTIST - FIRST EXAMPLE

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK SEVEN: THE FIGURE OF THE ARTIST - SECOND EXAMPLE

David Cronenberg's Crimes of the Future seems similar to Phantom Thread in having its central character, Saul Tenser, as an artist - a maker of art. Unlike Woodcock, the art he makes is himself but - perhaps paradoxically - his control over it is minimal. This theme, of art as a kind of involuntary gift (or 'syndrome') has some pedigree in the world of humanities criticism - the gifted artist. We will consider how some of the central themes of authorship and its relationship to the social and political world (as well as the world of the audience, the consumers of it) is handled effectively in the film.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK SEVEN: THE FIGURE OF THE ARTIST - SECOND EXAMPLE

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK EIGHT: TONE, FRIENDSHIP AND VIOLENCE AS AESTHETIC FEATURES

We have looked carefully so far in the course at complex and amorphous things like the artist-character, the spectacle of aesthetic experience, the distinction between criticism led by the authority of criteria or criticism that accrues its authority from a close reading of the film itself. But what about cinema's capacity to show, represent and capture other aspects of congealed human experience? Michael Cimino's The Deer Hunter is an attempt to get to grips with nature of friendship under extreme conditions: how successful do we think this is, what dramatic or artistic (rather than documentary) truth is there in its handling of friendship in the face of violence?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK EIGHT: TONE, FRIENDSHIP AND VIOLENCE AS AESTHETIC FEATURES

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK NINE: VIOLENCE AS VALUE

This week we continue our look at one of the most direct forms of aesthetic engagement - fictional violence. It is one of the most prominent and controversial ways in which film technique, style and methods of realistic or gratuitous depiction continues to be discussed. This week we look at the notorious example of Reservoir Dogs - even more so than The Deer Hunter this is a film that is self-conscious about its methods, themes and forms of address that entangle the viewer in impossible kinds of allegiance. To what extent is there a moral cost or price to be paid for our aesthetic involvement?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK ELEVEN: ACTING AND BULLSHIT

The conceit of The Man Who Wasn't There is to present a central protagonist, Ed Crane, who seems utterly devoid of any desire, quite unlike the usual film noir protagonist. This passive figure provides us with a fine opportunity to consider the achievement and significance of acting as another primary form of engagement with evaluation. After all so much of our judgement - as this course has already demonstrated - is caught up in whether or not we find the performances in the films convincing, thrilling, engaging, successful, and so on. Who is responsible for the performance - the actor alone? the director? the script? the editing? our own history as consumers of art?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK TWELVE: ACTING AND THE LEGACY OF ROBERT BRESSON

Robert Bresson is one of the most revered film directors not least because his handling of actors (many of whom are non professional) is distinctive; we continue our interest in acting, performance and the handling of character interiority by looking at the ways Bressonian acting gets inherited in contemporary cinema. Among the closest to Bresson's style are the Belgian directors and brothers Jean-Pierre and Luc Dardenne and this week we will look at their 2019 film Young Ahmed and explore the ways it handles the depiction and avoidance of interiority.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK TWELVE: ACTING AND THE LEGACY OF ROBERT BRESSON

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK THIRTEEN: AESTHETICS AND SPEED

This week we look at one of defining medium-specific aspects of cinema, its depiction of movement, especially speed. In looking at one of the most visually dynamic films of the past decade (Top Gun: Maverick) we will pull together the various strands of the course: medium-specific criteria/close textual reading/aesthetics as spectacle/the figure of the artist/friendship/violence/interiority and acting as well as tone.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
